question: Why guard against empty list before division?
options: len([]) == 0 causes ZeroDivisionError on the division
explain: Dividing by len([]) raises ZeroDivisionError. Guard aggregate functions against empty inputs before dividing or indexing. The defensive programming principle requires explicit handling of boundary cases.

question: Why === over ==?
options: Strict equality avoids type coercion surprises
explain: == performs type coercion which is flagged by ESLint eqeqeq. Using block-scoped declarations and strict equality reduces hoisting surprises and coercion bugs. The review principle of explicit comparison surfaces type mismatches early.

question: Why parameterized query vs string formatting?
options: Defends against SQL injection by separating data from code
explain: User-controlled input must never be interpolated into SQL text. Parameterized queries with placeholders let the driver handle escaping, preserving the value/code boundary. This is the canonical defense against CWE-89 injection vulnerabilities.

question: Why iterator combinators over an indexed loop?
options: Slices are more general than &Vec; iterators elide bounds checks
explain: Idiomatic Rust prefers slices over &Vec and iterator combinators over index loops. Programming to the most general borrowed type and leveraging the iterator API improves reuse and clarity while letting the optimizer prove bounds checks unnecessary.

code:
SELECT department, AVG(salary) AS avg_sal
FROM employees
WHERE salary IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY department
HAVING AVG(salary) > 50000;
question: Why filter NULL salaries before AVG?
options: AVG skips NULL by default; explicit filter documents intent and excludes all-NULL groups
explain: Aggregate functions like AVG ignore NULL inputs and a customer with all-NULL salaries yields AVG = NULL which fails the predicate. Explicitly handling NULL in aggregates with COALESCE or filtering upfront makes the contract clear to readers.
